mnemonics.title: TCP Handshake Steps
mnemonics.content: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK. Remember: 'SYN' sounds like 'SIN' - you sin to establish a connection (SYN), get forgiveness (SYN-ACK), and then are ACKnologized!

mnemonics.title: TCP Flags
mnemonics.content: C(ontrol) U(gent) R(eset) S(yn) F(in) P(ush) A(cknowledge). Remember: 'CURS PA' - like you're CURSed to PAy attention to flags!

mnemonics.title: Flow vs Congestion Control
mnemonics.content: Flow Control = Receiver's fault (buffer full). Congestion Control = Network's fault (routers overloaded). FLOW = FReighted LOad (receiver side), CONGESTION = CONtainer (network) issues.

mnemonics.title: TCP Header Fields
mnemonics.content: SourCe Port, Dest Port, Seq Num, Ack Num, Offset, Res, Flags, Window, Checksum, Urgent, Options, Data. Remember: 'SDAA OR WA COU D' - Sounds like a person named 'Sada Orwacoud'!

mnemonics.title: Congestion Control Phases
mnemonics.content: Slow Start → Congestion Avoidance. Remember: 'Start SLOW, then CAUTIOUS' - like learning to drive!

acronyms.usage: The largest TCP payload size that can be sent without fragmentation, typically 1460 bytes on Ethernet.
Time wait before retransmitting an unacknowledged segment, dynamically calculated using RTT measurements.
Time for a packet to travel to destination and back, crucial for timeout calculations.
Sender's limit on unacknowledged data, controlled by congestion control algorithms.
Threshold value determining whether to use slow start or congestion avoidance phase.
TCP option allowing receiver to acknowledge non-contiguous blocks of data, improving retransmission efficiency.
Largest packet size a network can transmit, typically 1500 bytes for Ethernet including headers.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a timeline with Client on left and Server on right. Client sends SYN→ (arrow right), Server responds SYN-ACK← (arrow left), Client sends ACK→. Number the sequence numbers: x, y, x+1, y+1. Color-code each step differently.

Draw a horizontal bar divided into three sections: 'Sent-Acked' (left, gray), 'Sent-Not-Acked' (middle, yellow), 'Usable Window' (right, green). Show arrows pointing right as ACKs arrive, making the window 'slide' forward.

Plot cwnd (y-axis) vs Time (x-axis). Show exponential rise in Slow Start as steep curve, then linear rise in Congestion Avoidance as shallow slope. Mark 'ssthresh' as horizontal line. Show 'sawtooth' pattern with drops.

Draw a 32-bit row structure: Row 1: Source Port | Dest Port; Row 2: Sequence Number; Row 3: Ack Number; Row 4: Offset | Flags | Window; Row 5: Checksum | Urgent Ptr; Row 6: Options | Data. Use different colors for each field.

analogies.analogy: TCP is like a phone call: You dial (SYN), the other person answers and confirms (SYN-ACK), you say 'hello' (ACK). You talk and confirm understanding throughout ('Got it' = ACK). If you don't hear response, you repeat (retransmit). Finally, you say goodbye (FIN) and hang up.

analogies.concept: TCP Sliding Window as a Moving Conveyor Belt
analogies.analization: Imagine a conveyor belt with packages (segments). The 'acknowledged' packages have moved off. The 'in-flight' packages are in the middle section. The 'usable window' is like empty slots at the end ready for new packages. As acknowledgments arrive, the belt slides forward bringing new slots into view.

analogies.concept: Flow Control as a Water Balloon
analogies.analogy: Flow control is like filling a water balloon - the receiver has a limited capacity (buffer). The sender needs to wait for the receiver to 'acknowledge' they've emptied some space before sending more water (data), otherwise it will overflow (data loss).
